subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 10! This unit is all about calculating – working out answers to addition, subtraction and multiplication problems in clever, quick ways. You already know how to count and how to read numbers. Now you will learn to be a real problem-solver who can add and take away two-digit numbers, split numbers into friendly parts, double and halve, and see multiplication as groups. These are skills you will use every single day of your life.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 2 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Recall number bonds to 10 and to 20 quickly and use them to help you calculate.
Add two two-digit numbers using partitioning (splitting into tens and ones).
Subtract by counting back, counting up, and partitioning.
Use rounding and adjusting (compensation) to add and subtract near numbers such as 9, 19 and 29.
Understand subtraction as the inverse of addition and use it to check your answers.
Double and halve numbers, and know that these two moves undo each other.
Understand multiplication as repeated addition, equal groups and arrays.
Skip count in twos, fives and tens to help with multiplication.
Choose a sensible method and estimate to check that your answer is reasonable.

concepts.title: Number Bonds – The Building Blocks
concepts.explanation: Before we add and subtract big numbers, we need to know our number bonds by heart. A number bond is a pair of numbers that join together to make a total. The most useful bonds of all are the bonds to ten, because ten is such a friendly, round number. Here they are: 0 + 10, 1 + 9, 2 + 8, 3 + 7, 4 + 6, 5 + 5, and then the same pairs the other way around. When Farah knows instantly that 6 + 4 = 10, she can calculate far more quickly than a friend who has to count every time. Why do bonds to ten matter so much?…
concepts.example: Once you are sure of your bonds to ten, you can stretch them to make bonds to twenty: 11 + 9, 12 + 8, 13 + 7, and so on. You can also use bonds to find what is missing. If Hassan has 10 sweets to share and gives 6 away,…

concepts.title: Adding Two-Digit Numbers by Partitioning
concepts.explanation: To partition a number means to split it into its tens and its ones. The number 34 partitions into 30 and 4. The number 25 partitions into 20 and 5. Partitioning turns a big, scary-looking calculation into a few small, friendly ones. It is the most important addition strategy in this whole unit. Let us add 34 + 25 together, step by step. First, partition both numbers: 34 becomes 30 and 4; 25 becomes 20 and 5. Next, add the tens: 30 + 20 = 50. Then add the ones: 4 + 5 = 9. Finally, put the two answers back together:…
concepts.example: Here is another way many children like, called adding in two jumps or ‘keep one number whole’. To do 47 + 36, keep 47 whole and partition only the 36. First add the 30: 47 + 30 = 77. Then add the 6: 77 + 6 = 83. Notice…

concepts.title: Rounding and Adjusting (Compensation)
concepts.explanation: Some numbers are almost round, and that makes them easy to work with if we are a little bit clever. Think about adding 9. Adding 9 is very close to adding 10, and 10 is a friendly round number. So instead of adding 9 the hard way, we add 10 and then take 1 back. This clever trick is called compensation, or rounding and adjusting. Let us try 47 + 9. Round the 9 up to 10: 47 + 10 = 57. But we added one too many, so we adjust by taking 1 away: 57 − 1 = 56. Check it feels right: 47 + 9 should be close to 47 + 10, and…
concepts.example: The same idea works for adding 19, 29 or any number near a ten. To add 19, add 20 and take 1 back. To add 29, add 30 and take 1 back. So Omar working out 34 + 19 thinks: 34 + 20 = 54, then 54 − 1 = 53. Rounding up and…

concepts.title: Subtraction and Its Methods
concepts.explanation: Subtraction means taking away or finding the difference between two numbers. Just like addition, there are several good methods, and you choose the one that fits the numbers. Let us meet three friendly methods. Method 1 – Counting back. This works well when you are taking away a small amount. For 58 − 3, start at 58 and count back three: 57, 56, 55. Your finger hops backwards along the number line one step at a time.
concepts.example: Method 2 – Partitioning. For 68 − 25, partition the 25 into 20 and 5. First take away the 20: 68 − 20 = 48. Then take away the 5: 48 − 5 = 43. Splitting the number you subtract into tens and ones makes it manageable,…

concepts.title: Subtraction as the Inverse of Addition
concepts.explanation: Here is one of the most powerful ideas in all of calculating: addition and subtraction are inverses. That word ‘inverse’ means opposite – each one undoes the other. If you add 5 and then subtract 5, you are back where you started. This connection gives you a marvellous way to check your work. Suppose you work out 47 − 23 = 24. How do you know you are right? Use the inverse! Add your answer back: 24 + 23 should give you 47. Let us check: 24 + 23 = 47. It matches, so your subtraction was correct. Every subtraction…
concepts.example: This idea also gives us fact families. From the three numbers 3, 5 and 8 we can build four facts: 3 + 5 = 8, 5 + 3 = 8, 8 − 3 = 5, and 8 − 5 = 3. Knowing one fact means you really know all four, because they are just…

concepts.title: Doubling and Halving
concepts.explanation: Doubling a number means adding it to itself, which is the same as multiplying by two. Double 4 is 4 + 4 = 8. Double 6 is 6 + 6 = 12. Doubling is a fast, useful skill, and the doubles up to double 10 are worth learning by heart, just like number bonds: double 1 is 2, double 2 is 4, double 3 is 6, and so on up to double 10 is 20. Halving is the inverse of doubling – it splits a number into two equal parts. Half of 8 is 4, because 4 and 4 make 8. Half of 12 is 6. Because doubling and halving undo each other, knowing…
concepts.example: Doubles also help with near doubles. If you know double 6 is 12, then 6 + 7 is just one more, so 6 + 7 = 13. And 6 + 5 is one less than double 6, so it is 11. Spotting a near double turns a fresh calculation into an…

methods.title: How to add two two-digit numbers
steps: Partition both numbers into tens and ones.
Add the tens together.
Add the ones together.
If the ones make more than ten, carry the extra ten across.
Join the tens and ones back together for your answer.

methods.title: How to subtract by counting up
steps: Start at the smaller number.
Count up to the next friendly ten.
Count up from there to the larger number.
Add your jumps together – that is the difference.

methods.title: How to multiply with equal groups
steps: Find how many groups there are and how many are in each group.
Write it as groups × size, such as 4 × 5.
Add the groups (repeated addition) or skip count to find the total.

methods.title: Subtract by counting up
steps: Solution: The two numbers are close together, so count up from 58 to 63.
From 58 to 60 is 2, and from 60 to 63 is 3.
Add the jumps: 2 + 3 = 5.

methods.title: Subtract by partitioning and check with the inverse
methods.example: Work out 76 − 34 and check your answer.
steps: Solution: Partition the 34 into 30 and 4.
Take away the 30: 76 − 30 = 46.
Take away the 4: 46 − 4 = 42.
Now check using the inverse: 42 + 34 should give 76.

methods.title: Double a two-digit number
steps: Solution: Partition 16 into 10 and 6.
Double the 10 to get 20, and double the 6 to get 12.
Join them: 20 + 12 = 32.
So double 16 is 32.

workedExamples.title: Add by partitioning
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Partition both numbers. 42 = 40 + 2 and 36 = 30 + 6. Add the tens: 40 + 30 = 70. Add the ones: 2 + 6 = 8. Join them back together: 70 + 8 = 78.

workedExamples.title: Add with regrouping
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Partition: 38 = 30 + 8 and 27 = 20 + 7. Tens: 30 + 20 = 50. Ones: 8 + 7 = 15. But 15 is one ten and five ones, so pass the ten across: 50 + 15 = 50 + 10 + 5 = 65.

workedExamples.title: Round and adjust
workedExamples.solution: Solution: 19 is close to 20, so add 20 and then take 1 back. 54 + 20 = 74. Now adjust: 74 − 1 = 73. Rounding up made the sum easy, and we paid back the extra 1 at the end.

workedExamples.title: Subtract by counting up
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The two numbers are close together, so count up from 58 to 63. From 58 to 60 is 2, and from 60 to 63 is 3. Add the jumps: 2 + 3 = 5. So 63 − 58 = 5.

workedExamples.title: Subtract by partitioning and check with the inverse
workedExamples.prompt: Work out 76 − 34 and check your answer.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Partition the 34 into 30 and 4. Take away the 30: 76 − 30 = 46. Take away the 4: 46 − 4 = 42. Now check using the inverse: 42 + 34 should give 76. 42 + 34 = 76. It matches, so the answer 42 is correct.

workedExamples.title: Double a two-digit number
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Partition 16 into 10 and 6. Double the 10 to get 20, and double the 6 to get 12. Join them: 20 + 12 = 32. So double 16 is 32. (Check by halving: half of 32 is 16.)

workedExamples.title: Multiplication as repeated addition
workedExamples.prompt: Yusuf has 4 plates and puts 5 dates on each plate. How many dates altogether?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: This is 4 groups of 5, written 4 × 5. As repeated addition it is 5 + 5 + 5 + 5. Skip count in fives: 5, 10, 15, 20. So there are 20 dates.

workedExamples.title: Use an array and the swapping rule
workedExamples.prompt: Draw 3 rows of 6 stones. How many stones? What multiplication does it show, and what is the swapped fact?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: 3 rows of 6 is 3 × 6. Skip count in sixes or add 6 + 6 + 6 = 18 stones. The array shows 3 × 6 = 18. Turned on its side it becomes 6 rows of 3, so the swapped fact is 6 × 3 = 18. Both give the same total, 18.

workedExamples.title: Subtract by counting up
workedExamples.prompt: Subtract by counting up: a) 52 − 48 b) 71 − 66.

workedExamples.title: Skip count to solve
workedExamples.prompt: Skip count to solve: a) 5 × 2 b) 4 × 5 c) 3 × 10.

workedExamples.title: Practice 12
workedExamples.prompt: Work out 83 − 47 and check it using the inverse addition. Show both.

activities.title: Bonds-to-Ten Bean Flip
activities.materials: ten beans (or stones) and a small cloth.
steps: Count out exactly ten beans and place them on the cloth.
Hide some beans under the cloth and leave the rest showing.
Look at the beans showing and say how many are hidden, using your bonds to ten.
Lift the cloth to check. Play until you can name the hidden number instantly.
This trains the bonds to ten that make all your adding faster.

activities.title: Partition Party (Tens and Ones)
activities.materials: small sticks or beans, and some string or rubber bands.
steps: Choose a two-digit number, such as 34, and build it with bundles of ten and loose ones.
Choose a second number, such as 25, and build that too.
Add the tens bundles together, then the loose ones together.
Count the total and say the full calculation out loud: ‘34 plus 25 equals 59.’

activities.title: The Shopkeeper’s Change Game
activities.materials: paper ‘shilling’ coins and small items to ‘sell’.
steps: Put price labels on a few items, such as 15, 22 and 34 shillings.
One person is the shopper and pays with a larger note, such as 40 shillings.
The shopkeeper counts up from the price to the note to find the change.
Check the change by adding it back to the price. Swap roles and play again.
This is exactly how counting up to find a difference works in real life.

activities.title: Array Hunt Investigation
steps: Walk around your home and the masjid looking for arrays – tiles, windows, bricks, prayer mats in rows.
For each array you find, count the rows and the columns.
Write the multiplication it shows, such as ‘3 rows of 4 is 3 × 4 = 12’.
Turn the array in your mind and write the swapped fact too.

activities.title: Double or Halve Dice (Math Game)
activities.materials: one die (or number cards 1 to 10) and two players.
steps: Take turns to roll the die or pick a card.
One round everyone doubles the number; the next round everyone halves it.
Say your answer, and check a double by halving it back, or a halve by doubling it back.
Score a point for each correct answer. First to ten points wins.

activities.title: Skip-Count Family Game
activities.materials: your family or friends sitting in a circle.
steps: Choose a step size together: twos, fives or tens.
Go around the circle, each person saying the next number in the count.
If someone says the wrong number, the count starts again from the beginning.
See how high your family can skip count together without a mistake.

After your activities, talk or write about: Which activity helped you understand calculating best? Which method – partitioning, rounding and adjusting, or counting up – felt most comfortable? What would you like more practice with? Share your answers with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: Partitioning Rule
rules.text: Split each number into tens and ones. Add the tens, add the ones, then join them back together. 34 + 25 = (30 + 20) + (4 + 5) = 50 + 9 = 59.

rules.title: Rounding and Adjusting Rule
rules.text: To add 9, add 10 and take 1 back. To add 19, add 20 and take 1 back. To subtract 9, take 10 away and give 1 back. Whatever you change to make it round, undo it at the end.

rules.title: Inverse Rule
rules.text: Addition and subtraction undo each other. Check a subtraction by adding the answer back on. Double and halve also undo each other.

terms: Two numbers that join to make a total
Split a number into tens and ones
The answer to an addition / a subtraction
The opposite operation that undoes another
Round a number, then adjust the answer
Times two / split into two equal parts
Objects in equal rows and columns
A sensible guess to check an answer

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Work out 83 − 47 and check it using the inverse addition. Show both.
Double 24 by partitioning it into tens and ones. Show your steps.
Draw a 4 × 6 array. How many objects does it hold? Write the swapped multiplication fact.
Use a double you know to work out the near double 7 + 8. Explain your reasoning.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Double 7 = 14, and 8 is one more, so 7 + 8 = 14 + 1 = 15.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Amina says 40 + 30 = 7. What mistake has she made, and what is the correct answer?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: She added the tens and ones as if they were the same; 40 + 30 = 70, not 7.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Doubling and Halving.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Doubling a number means adding it to itself, which is the same as multiplying by two. Double 4 is 4 + 4 = 8. Double 6 is 6 + 6 = 12. Doubling is a fast, useful skill, and the doubles up to double 10 are worth learning by heart, just like number bonds: double 1 is 2, double 2 is 4, double 3 is 6, and so on up to double 10 is 20. Halving is the inverse of doubling – it splits a number into two equal parts. Half of 8 is 4, because 4 and 4 make 8. Half of 12 is 6. Because doubling and halving undo each other, knowing…

questions.question: Work out 42 + 36.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 10 methods.
questions.explanation: Partition both numbers. 42 = 40 + 2 and 36 = 30 + 6. Add the tens: 40 + 30 = 70. Add the ones: 2 + 6 = 8. Join them back together: 70 + 8 = 78.

questions.question: Work out 38 + 27.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 10 methods.
questions.explanation: Partition: 38 = 30 + 8 and 27 = 20 + 7. Tens: 30 + 20 = 50. Ones: 8 + 7 = 15. But 15 is one ten and five ones, so pass the ten across: 50 + 15 = 50 + 10 + 5 = 65.

questions.question: Work out 54 + 19.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 10 methods.
questions.explanation: 19 is close to 20, so add 20 and then take 1 back. 54 + 20 = 74. Now adjust: 74 − 1 = 73. Rounding up made the sum easy, and we paid back the extra 1 at the end.

questions.question: Work out 63 − 58.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 10 methods.
questions.explanation: The two numbers are close together, so count up from 58 to 63. From 58 to 60 is 2, and from 60 to 63 is 3. Add the jumps: 2 + 3 = 5. So 63 − 58 = 5.

questions.question: Work out 76 − 34 and check your answer.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 10 methods.
questions.explanation: Partition the 34 into 30 and 4. Take away the 30: 76 − 30 = 46. Take away the 4: 46 − 4 = 42. Now check using the inverse: 42 + 34 should give 76. 42 + 34 = 76. It matches, so the answer 42 is correct.

questions.question: Work out double 16.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 10 methods.
questions.explanation: Partition 16 into 10 and 6. Double the 10 to get 20, and double the 6 to get 12. Join them: 20 + 12 = 32. So double 16 is 32. (Check by halving: half of 32 is 16.)

questions.question: Work out 22 ÷ 2.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

questions.question: Work out 32 ÷ 8.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

questions.question: Work out 19 + 42.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

rounds.explanation: Partition 16 into 10 and 6. Double the 10 to get 20, and double the 6 to get 12. Join them: 20 + 12 = 32. So double 16 is 32. (Check by halving: half of 32 is 16.)

selfAssessment: I can recall number bonds to 10 and to 20 quickly and use them to help you calculate.
I can add two two-digit numbers using partitioning (splitting into tens and ones).
I can subtract by counting back, counting up, and partitioning.
I can use rounding and adjusting (compensation) to add and subtract near numbers such as 9, 19 and 29.
I can understand subtraction as the inverse of addition and use it to check your answers.
I can double and halve numbers, and know that these two moves undo each other.
I can understand multiplication as repeated addition, equal groups and arrays.
I can skip count in twos, fives and tens to help with multiplication.
